schools.summary: “After these things” is indefinite (a year on from ch.5); harmonises the four-Gospel feeding and fixes the lake’s names and the eastern Bethsaida.
Augustine: the feeding is a rarer but lesser wonder than daily providence (“He created as God creates”); Bede/Alcuin allegorise loaves=Law, fishes=priest/king, baskets=apostles.
Chrysostom: Christ withdrew to soften the Jews’ envy and gave thanks to teach us; Theophylact refutes the Manichees from the multiplied (hence good) bread.
Calvin: the crowd’s eager pursuit shames our sloth, and Christ feeds them before they ask, providing for those who neglect themselves to follow him.
Henry: Christ lived continually in a crowd, honoured the poor multitude, and questioned Philip only to prove him.
Wesley and Clarke supply the chronology bridged from the Synoptics.

debates.question: Did the disciples first urge dismissal (Synoptics) or did Jesus first question Philip (John)?

sides.position: one event, harmonised — Jesus asked Philip later, which John alone records
possibly two different occasions

outliers.note: Makes the miracle a meditation on providence — the daily government of the world is the greater, though commoner, miracle.

schools.summary: Imperfect tenses show continual following and healing; the crowd swelled from the Baptist’s death, the Twelve’s return, and Passover pilgrimage.
Calvin: their zeal, though it shames our sloth, rested on miracles — a weak faith convinced only that he was a great prophet.
“The mountain” = the eastern hill-country; the imperfect ‘sat’ pictures repose, with a later ascent to pray per Mark/Matthew.
Calvin: Christ sought solitude as man yet was led by God’s purpose into a ‘crowded theatre’ — we should plan by events and submit when results differ.
John’s explanatory aside; rabbinic 30-day Passover preparation; probably the third Passover, which Jesus did not attend (cf. 7:1) — and, for Ellicott, a key of interpretation to sign and discourse.
The Philip exchange is John’s own, ‘different but not opposed’ to the Synoptics; deliberative subjunctive, with Moses/Elisha echoes; Philip of Bethsaida had charge of food.
Calvin: Jesus spoke to Philip as voicing all, to arouse the disciples’ sleeping minds before the miracle.

debates.question: Who first raised the problem of feeding the crowd?

sides.position: the disciples (Synoptics) — John writes from the inner circle and does not contradict
Jesus, questioning Philip to test him

schools.summary: John’s own aside; ‘prove’ = test in the good sense (not tempt), not from ignorance but to draw out faith — and, for Ellicott, a model of Christ’s examining pedagogy.

outliers.note: Reads the proving as Christ’s educational method — moving the disciple from facts learned from without to the principle grasped from within.

schools.summary: 200 denarii = nearly a year’s wages, an impossible sum; Philip names it to show the human difficulty and ‘leaves it’ — a failure to reach divine sufficiency.
Calvin: the great sum is named to alarm Christ, meaning they had not the means to feed so many.
Identifies Andrew of the inner circle — first follower (1:40), paired with Philip, known for bringing Peter to Christ; the Synoptics keep the remark general.
Barley = inferior, slaves’ bread; opsaria = small relish-fish; the diminutives stress how little there was — and Lightfoot’s rabbinic parallel to Elisha shows Christ as the greater Prophet.
‘Recline’ on the green spring grass (fixing the Passover season); andres = the men only, besides women and children; seated in ranks of hundreds and fifties.
Calvin: the obedient seating, not knowing the outcome, is the trial of true faith — to walk at God’s bare word ‘as in darkness.’
Calvin: thanksgiving sanctifies food (to eat without God is sacrilege); Christ is the appointed channel of all blessing, yet feeds plainly and bids us live by God’s word.
‘Having given thanks’ = grace before meat (Synoptics say ‘blessed’); he distributed ‘as much as they would’; one loaf per thousand.
Cyril of Jerusalem reads the thanksgiving and breaking eucharistically — ‘this is My Body… My Blood’ — anticipating the discourse.

outliers.note: The lone sacramental reading, connecting the bread of the miracle to the institution of the Eucharist.

schools.summary: The klasmata are Christ’s broken pieces, gathered at his express command (John only); all draw the lesson of frugality even amid abundance — a rebuke to the rich.
Kophinoi (stout wicker baskets, all four Gospels), one per apostle, distinct from the spyrides of the four thousand.
Calvin: the surplus magnifies the blessing, yet the gathering still exhorts to frugality — bounty is no warrant for luxury.
‘That prophet’ = the Messiah of Deut 18:15; the sign joins the others, and the crowd expects a political, Pharisaic Messiah (cf. their earlier hope of the Baptist).
Henry and Calvin: a true confession turned at once to an improper purpose — men may own him as Prophet yet pervert the truth and not continue in it.
Henry: the crowd’s ‘irregular zeal’ — a worldly kingdom, fleshly motive, seditious design; Christ’s flight is humility against ambition and faction.
A temporal-Messiah misreading verging on anti-Roman revolution; Jesus hurried off the sympathetic disciples and withdrew to pray; no change comes by might (Clarke, Zech 4:6).
Calvin: appointing a king is God’s prerogative (Ps 2:6); the earthly kingship they devised is inconsistent with Christ’s person.
Augustine: he who fled kingship was already King, making men kings; flight and prayer agree. Chrysostom: ‘see what the belly can do.’

outliers.note: Develops the paradox of the true, heaven-derived kingship and reads the flight mystically as Christ’s ascension above all comprehension.

schools.summary: ‘Real’ late evening (not the mid-afternoon of Mt 14:15); destination Capernaum (John) / western Bethsaida (Mark).
Chrysostom: the named hour shows the disciples’ love — they could not endure not to seek their Master.
Calvin: Christ concealed himself to let the tumult disperse and prayed against the people’s folly; the crossing would confirm faith.
Graphic tenses (‘darkness had come, Jesus had not’); ‘the ship’ still crossing; harmonises Capernaum/Bethsaida/Gennesaret on the western shore, storm-driven.
Calvin: supplies the fourth-watch struggle; the danger is God’s way of making his people know him in their deliverance.
Imperfect ‘was rising’ (diegeireto) with genitive absolute ‘a great wind blowing’; Barnes frames it by Ps 107:25 — the LORD raises the storm.
Rowed about halfway across the ~6-mile lake; vivid present tenses; the fear (ingressive aorist) was of a supposed ghost per the Synoptics.
Calvin: bare sight of divinity without the word breeds idols and terror; only Christ’s voice gives solid knowledge and peace.

themes: knowledge of Christ by the word

schools.summary: Calvin: ‘It is I’ comforts the disciples but overwhelms the reprobate (cf. 18:6); to believers his name pledges God’s love and salvation, and he stills the storm.
Parallels in Mark/Matthew (which add Peter); Barnes hears the divine ‘fear not’ of Isa 43 and Rev 1:17.
Debate over whether the instant landing is a third miracle; Ellicott, Barnes, Robertson read ‘straightway’ as rapid not miraculous, against Clarke’s leaning.

debates.question: Is the sudden arrival at land a separate miracle?

sides.position: yes — the landing seems extraordinary and miraculous
no — ‘straightway’ means rapid, not instantaneous; a contrast of calm vs. storm, not a new wonder

schools.summary: Untangles the geography and logic by which the crowd inferred a miraculous crossing; Clarke argues peran = ‘by the side of,’ not ‘beyond.’
Chrysostom: they could infer he walked across, yet shallowly never ask how; Augustine: the miracle was known indirectly.
Henry: those who would find Christ must observe all his motions and seek till they find — though this crowd’s zeal was still carnal.
A parenthesis explaining the extra boats; Tiberias’ Herodian foundation and later rabbinic fame, with Barnes’ modern travelogue.
Calvin: the repeated ‘had given thanks’ stresses that prayer made the loaves suffice — pressed twice because we pray so coldly.
Logistics of the crossing (Lightfoot on the route and synagogue day); not all 5,000 sailed; Robertson reads the double, carnal motive — kingship and another meal.
The scene is the Capernaum synagogue (v.59), perhaps the centurion’s (Lk 7:5); ‘Rabbi, when camest thou?’ is puzzled amazement half-meaning ‘how,’ a wonder they will not pursue.
The 'given' are the elect, handed to Christ in the eternal covenant; their coming is certain and none is lost.
Collective neuter + strong double negative = a sure welcome; the gift draws but does not compel.
The given are those who feel lost and follow the Father's drawing; 'cast out' = no penitent ever refused.

debates.question: Who are 'all that the Father giveth me'?

sides.position: the fixed company of the elect
all who yield to the Father's drawing

outliers.note: Holds sovereign gift and free will in deliberate union, citing Bernard and the Tenth Article rather than choosing a side.

schools.summary: The Son's sole design is the Father's will, so he cannot reject those the Father sends; the 'own will' language is accommodation to our distrust.
Perfect 'have come down' + the joy of doing the Father's will guarantees the promise of v.37.
Not his own will = not human prejudice that would exclude sinners and Gentiles, but mercy that receives them.

outliers.note: Stresses that 'came down from heaven' is no local motion but the assumption of human nature.

themes: union of Father and Son's will

schools.summary: 'Lose nothing' = final perseverance; Christ keeps the elect to the last resurrection.
The keeping is conditional on enduring; God willed Judas might be saved without willing that he must.
'The last day' is a fourfold refrain; only the resurrection of the righteous is in view here.

debates.question: Is 'I should lose nothing' an unconditional securing of the elect, or conditioned on perseverance?

sides.position: unconditional final perseverance
conditional on enduring (Judas the proof)

outliers.note: Sets the verse against the Jewish hope of resurrection at Messiah's coming and their denial of it to the wilderness generation.

schools.summary: Seeing is not enough — faith is required and is the present possession of eternal life; the 'all' narrows to 'every one'.
This is the revealed will that executes the secret decree; faith itself attests election, and prying beyond it insults the Spirit.
God's unchangeable will that every believer be saved; he gives power to believe but does not believe for us.

outliers.note: Argues that this verse, rightly heeded, prevents the abuse of election into fatalistic inactivity.

schools.summary: Chrysostom: the offence was carnal disappointment, not real difficulty; they call him 'son of Joseph' not yet knowing his birth.
Augustine: they lacked the inner hunger for the bread/righteousness of heaven; the incarnation is real but not after the manner of men.
Calvin: they stumbled at the meanness of his human condition, blind to the glory veiled in his self-emptying.
Robertson/Ellicott: the buzzing 'murmur' of authorities fastening on what they will not understand.
His ordinary Galilean origin was well known; they reason 'it is human, therefore it cannot be divine.'
Gill: the murmur descends into a scoff at his lowly birth, meant to shame his heavenly claims.

outliers.note: The JFB material merged at this verse comments on another scene and does not address v.42.

schools.summary: 'Stop murmuring' (present imperative) stems a rising protest; Jesus does not answer their difficulty but resumes the discourse.
Calvin: the offence proves a vitiated, reprobate taste, not a fault in the doctrine.
The drawing is an efficacious, sovereign impulse of the Spirit that makes the unwilling willing; the willingness itself is God's gift.
The drawing is real but resistible — alluring not dragging; God inclines the will yet man yields without compulsion.
'helkyō' = to draw by moral power (cf. John 12:32); the soul's approach is initiated by God.
Augustine: we are drawn by delight — to come unwillingly would be not to believe at all.

debates.question: Is the Father's 'drawing' efficacious and irresistible, or real but resistible?

sides.position: efficacious, sovereign, making the unwilling willing
real but resistible, alluring not compelling

outliers.note: Resolves the tension by 'drawn by delight' (trahit sua quemque voluptas), held by Reformed and Arminian alike.

schools.summary: The 'teaching of God' is the inward illumination of the Spirit, efficacious so that the taught necessarily believe; 'all' = the elect.
Isaiah 54:13 quoted; the teaching is non-compulsory, requiring a voluntary hearing and learning, yet such a one comes to Christ.
God teaches by love, showing a man his need; only the one who hears the inward voice believes.

debates.question: Does 'all shall be taught of God' name a universal teaching freely offered, or the effectual instruction of the elect?

sides.position: effectual, particular teaching of the elect
inward illumination open to free response

outliers.note: Reads Isaiah's 'taught of God' against the rabbis who made it the disciples of the wise.

schools.summary: Christ's claim to have seen the Father implies deity — the only-begotten from God who alone reveals him (John 1:18; 14:9).
No knowledge of God is had apart from Christ; knowledge sought outside him is 'a deadly abyss.'
Cyril: even angels see God only as they can bear; the Son, with the Spirit, beholds the Father fully and reveals him by measure.

outliers.note: Grades the vision of God — angels by capacity, the Son fully — guarding the verse against Matthew 18:10.

schools.summary: Augustine: to believe is to have Christ, who is life; the sacrament differs from its virtue — spiritual eating brings an innocent mind to the altar.
Faith is the organ of life because all of life is in Christ; believing and eating differ as cause and effect.
Eternal life is the believer's present possession, not future only (John 5:24).

outliers.note: Frames the verse by the manna/living-bread contrast: temporal benefit vs never dying.

schools.summary: A deliberate repetition of v.35: the unseen life of God borne and imparted in the visible human nature of the Son.
Calvin: the repetition sets up the renewed contrast with the manna and the men who ate it.
Clarke: Christ alone, by doctrine and Spirit, gives the nourishment that saves to eternal life.

outliers.note: The JFB note merged here concerns a temple-cleansing scene and does not bear on v.48.

schools.summary: Clarke argues from the Jews' own belief that the wilderness generation, though they ate manna, would not rise — so manna is not the bread of life.
Temporal blessing cannot save from death; the manna did not prevent death, the true bread prevents spiritual death.
Calvin guards the manna as a figure of Christ (1 Cor 10:3) while telling the belly-minded it was perishing food; eaters of Christ never lose life.

outliers.note: Notes the pointed 'your fathers' — those who ate manna as common food, not as a type of Messiah.

schools.summary: Wesley: 'not die' = not spiritually, not eternally — distinct from the bodily death of v.49.
The purpose clause names the bread (Christ himself) whose eating secures a life death cannot hold; 'beyond the insight of the crowd.'
The flesh given on the cross and in the sacrament; Theophylact realist ('very flesh'), Augustine spiritualizing it into incorporation into Christ's body.
'My flesh... for the life of the world' = the atoning death of the true Paschal Lamb at the coming Passover.
Calvin: the flesh is the channel, not the source, of life; the verse condemns the Mass's usurpation of the one High Priest's work.
Wesley: the words refer directly to the passion, 'remotely, if at all, to the Lord's Supper.'

debates.question: Does 'my flesh' point primarily to the sacrament or to the atoning death?

sides.position: both the incarnate/sacramental flesh, read realistically then spiritually
the atoning death/cross, not the Supper

outliers.note: Reads the 'flesh to eat' as wholly parabolic, against the Jewish horror of eating man's flesh and blood.

schools.summary: Augustine/Bede: the strivers misread him as dividing his flesh; those who truly eat are made one, not contentious.
The imperfect 'strove' = a rising war of words; their scornful 'How can?' echoes Nicodemus.
Calvin: asking 'how' is lawful (so did Abraham and Mary); the sin is the quarrelsome hardihood that blocks the road to truth.

outliers.note: Reads the strife as the cue for Christ to make the eating not merely possible but necessary.

schools.summary: The eating is the spiritual appropriation of Christ by faith, not the literal sacrament (instituted a year later; the unworthy perish).
Calvin: the verse concerns the ongoing eating of Christ's flesh by faith, not the Supper; the ancients erred in using it for infant communion.
Chrysostom presses the eating as necessary and answers the death-objection by the Resurrection; Cyril/Origen read the blood of Spirit and new covenant.
Augustine: distinguishes the sacrament from its reality — only one within the body's unity truly eats and lives.

debates.question: Does 'eat my flesh, drink my blood' speak of the Lord's Supper or of feeding on Christ by faith?

sides.position: spiritual feeding by faith, not the Supper
sacramental/realist, eaten within the Church's unity

outliers.note: Sets the blood among the Word's many forms — water, wine, blood, living bread — given as each can receive.

schools.summary: The stronger 'trōgōn' is present/habitual eating = continual feeding on Christ by faith; the sacramental reading is rejected as misinterpretation.
Calvin: souls feed on Christ as the body on bread; the Supper seals this sermon but is not its subject, since many communicate to condemnation.
Clarke: a triple disproof that the verse means the Supper (not yet instituted; non-reception not damning; unworthy receivers perish).

debates.question: Is the Lord's Supper the subject of this verse?

sides.position: no — habitual feeding on Christ by faith; the Supper only seals it
the Supper is at least sacramentally in view

outliers.note: The JFB material merged here concerns the temple cleansing and does not bear on v.54.

schools.summary: A true partaking, understood spiritually: Hilary grounds mutual indwelling in it; Theophylact, the Word-united flesh quickens 'as heated iron.'
Read 'true food/true drink'; as the true vine and true bread, Christ truly nourishes to eternal life.
Calvin: the soul perishes without this bread; nothing is excellent but Christ crucified, against those who draw souls from his flesh.

outliers.note: Makes the true eating the basis of a 'natural' mutual indwelling of believer and Christ, and of Christ and the Father.

schools.summary: 'Abideth in me and I in him' = continual mystical fellowship (menō), the abiding home-life with Christ, not the Supper.
Calvin: the bond is faith resting on Christ's death; the outward symbol unbelievers share leaves them separate — so Judas did not receive Christ.
Clarke: as food becomes the eater's substance, so Christ and the believer indwell each other, partakers of the divine nature.
Calvin's 'three degrees of life': the hidden Father as source, the Son as open fountain, the life we draw — said of Christ in the flesh, not his bare Divinity.
'Because of the Father... because of me': the Father is life's source, Christ the believer's ground of immortality (John 5:26; 14:19).
Clarke: the believer's union with Christ mirrors the Son's union with the Father.
A summary close: the past 'came down' points to his historic coming; the discourse ends on its own text — Christ is the true manna.
Calvin: Christ outdoes Moses; 'from heaven' = incorruptible, breathing the immortality of God's kingdom, not a physical descent.
Wesley: 'I am the bread,' unlike the manna whose eaters died notwithstanding.
Capernaum (Tell-Hûm): excavated synagogue with a carved pot of manna; the place and the interrupted discourse fully proclaim his passion.
Calvin: a weighty public sermon that scarcely any profited by, becoming the occasion of mass desertion — a warning never to murmur at Christ.
Augustine: the saying is 'hard' only to carnal hearing; 'to be carnally-minded is death' — the flesh must not be understood carnally.
Chrysostom/Theophylact: too lofty for their weakness; the murmurers were hangers-on, not true disciples.
Henry: had they meekly asked, he would have opened the parable; their unwillingness made the saying a pretext.
Calvin: a warning against being shaken when professed disciples desert in crowds.

outliers.note: The JFB note merged here is from another scene and does not bear on v.60.

schools.summary: Henry: Christ knows the secret murmur by his own omniscience, the Word that discerns the heart (Heb 4:12); the offence is self-created.
The murmuring was 'among themselves'; Jesus, knowing it within, meets it directly and gently.
His knowledge of secret cavils is a proof of his deity.
Chrysostom: the foretold ascension is a mark of his Divinity that removes doubt about his heavenly origin.
To see him ascend would prove he descended; the ascension is the crowning sign.
Henry weighs the senses: a harder test, or a removal of the gross literal eating, or the very proof that would satisfy (Eph 4:9-10).

debates.question: Is the ascension named to deepen the offence or to resolve it?

sides.position: a still-harder test of faith
the proof of his descent that removes the gross literal eating

outliers.note: Uses the verse for the unity of person — Son of man in heaven, Son of God on earth — against a 'quaternity.'

schools.summary: Chrysostom: 'the flesh profits nothing' is not his own flesh but the carnal hearer's mode of understanding.
Augustine: join spirit to flesh and it profits much — else the Word would not have become flesh; the flesh cleanses by the Word who assumed it.
'The flesh' = the carnal, literal mode of understanding; the Spirit gives life and Christ's words are spirit and life.
Henry's threefold sense: ordinance without the Spirit, literal vs spiritual reading, and the carnal who profit nothing (1 Cor 2:14).

sides.position: the carnal hearer / carnal mode of understanding
not Christ's own flesh — that is the Word made flesh

schools.summary: Henry: the answer to Isaiah 53:1; the hypocrite's unbelief is open to Christ, and a long professor's apostasy proves he never believed (1 John 2:19).
Christ's foreknowledge of the unbelieving and the betrayer 'shows his high nature.'
Augustine: 'except you believe you shall not understand'; he knew all before the foundation of the world.
Calvin reads the secret discrimination of elect from reprobate.
Augustine: faith is a gift — 'what have you that you did not receive?'; why it is not given to all rests with God's unsearchable judgments.
Coming is the Father's gift and faith his work, so Christ infallibly knows who believes; fallen man cannot come without it.
Clarke/Henry: none comes without the gift of grace and a heart to come, yet the unbelief remains the hearers' own.

debates.question: Is the Father's 'gift' to come efficacious for the elect, or grace offered to be received?

sides.position: efficacious gift distinguishing elect from reprobate
grace given to enable a free coming

schools.summary: Henry: a backsliding like Orpah's (Ruth 1:15), infectious, occasioned by the very doctrine of comfort; God's word must capture men's humours, not bend to them.
Augustine/Chrysostom: they 'went back' from a belief once held, cut off from the body, after Satan not Christ.
Calvin: the foundation of God stands sure though many fall; these were nominal disciples never truly believing.

outliers.note: The JFB material merged here is from another passage and does not bear on v.66.

schools.summary: Chrysostom: by showing he did not need them, Christ bound the twelve closer; he asks rather than dismisses, so none stays from shame or necessity.
Henry: a question of free choice — Christ's soldiers are volunteers (Josh 24:15) — yet at their peril, for nearness deepens the guilt of desertion.
The question tests and confirms the steadfastness of the twelve, casting none off.
Chrysostom: a speech of greatest love — Christ dearer than father or mother; Peter recalls 'I will raise him up... hath eternal life.'
Peter contrasts the words of eternal life with every alternative; the offending saying becomes the church's confession.
The genuine faith of the elect over against the recorded apostasy.

outliers.note: 'Give us another to whom we shall go, if we leave you' — the faith that clings for want of any other refuge.

schools.summary: Augustine: 'we believed in order to know' — the confession that he is eternal life who gives in his flesh what he is himself.
'We have believed and have come to know' — faith ripening into assurance, the foil to the murmurers.
Cyril sets 'Son of the living God' within the church's rule of faith (Matt 16:16).
The confession of true faith in the elect, answering Christ's question.
Chrysostom: a reproof that checks presumption yet veils the betrayer — neither shaming nor emboldening him.
Augustine/Bede: God turns Judas's evil to the greatest good; the eleven chosen to apostleship, the twelfth to the service of betrayal, election leaving the will free.
The shock of a chosen one called 'a devil'; chosen to office, not to salvation.
Wesley: election to apostleship imposed no necessity to sin.

debates.question: How can one 'chosen' by Christ be a devil?

sides.position: distinguish the ends of election — apostleship vs salvation, evil turned to good
election to office leaves the will free; the sin is Judas's own

schools.summary: The pointed 'one of the twelve' heightens both Christ's foreknowledge and the horror — the betrayer from the chosen band, not the deserting crowd.
Calvin/Gill: the naming confirms Christ knew his own from the first; the betrayal serves, not thwarts, redemption.
‘Signs’ not ‘miracles’: the crowd grasped the wonder but not its spiritual sense; a sharp, deserved rebuke of belly over soul (Ellicott, Robertson, Barnes).
Calvin: their sin is seeking in Christ something other than Christ himself — ease in this world rather than surrender and the kingdom.
Chrysostom: Christ rebukes sparingly, not abusively, seeking not honour but their salvation; Cyril lists the scriptural ways to eternal life.
Wesley: the ministry’s hinge — Christ now tries the hearers’ sincerity by the figurative discourse on his passion.

outliers.note: Turns from the rebuke to the remedy, cataloguing how Scripture says eternal life is gained — by faith and by gospel preaching.

themes: seeking Christ for the wrong motive

schools.summary: ‘Work not’ (not ‘labour not’) links to vv.28–30; the command forbids anxiety, not honest work; the seal = the Father’s attestation, esp. at the baptism; Lightfoot: ‘the seal of God is Truth’ = Christ.
Clarke: labour cannot purchase heaven, yet ‘he that will have heaven must strive for it’ — the appointed way God gives it.

outliers.note: Rabbinic background: ‘the seal of God is Truth,’ applied to Christ the Truth (John 14:6), ratified by the Father’s seal.

themes: the Father’s sealing of the Son

schools.summary: The sinner’s instinct to merit by works (Barnes); ‘works’ (plural) vs the coming ‘work’ (singular); Lightfoot’s labourer-eats-of-his-work background — believe and feed on Christ.
Augustine: believe ‘in Him,’ not merely ‘Him’ (the devils did the latter) — faith that loves, honours, is incorporated, and works by love.
Chrysostom: gluttony makes the mind gross so they grovel below despite Christ’s many sayings; Theophylact: he answered for others’ later good.
Calvin: they speak without understanding, ignorant of the grace God gives by the hand of the Son.
Henry: a humble question; Christ’s condescension models teachers ‘swift to hear.’

outliers.note: The classic ‘believe Him’ vs ‘believe in Him’ distinction — saving faith as love, honour, and incorporation into Christ’s Body.

schools.summary: Faith is THE work of God, ‘the complete solution of faith and works’ (Westcott); faith and work are one, faith working by love (Gal 5:6); believing saves (Rom 10:4).
Calvin: faith alone is required (he denies this verse proves faith a gift — it shows what God requires, not what he produces); yet faith excludes neither love nor good works.
Wesley: faith is the work most pleasing to God and the foundation of all others.

debates.question: Does ‘the work of God’ here mean faith as God’s gift, or faith as what God requires of us?

sides.position: God’s own operation — faith is the pure gift of God
what God requires from us, not what he produces in us

themes: faith as the work of God

schools.summary: They demand a sign the day after the loaves — zeal soon cooled, hearts on perishing things; perhaps synagogue rulers who saw no miracle; a demand to match Christ’s spiritual claim, setting up the manna contrast.
The crowd invokes the manna to demand a ‘greater Moses’ sign (rabbinic Ps 72:16 expectation); their fathers’ heaven-sent manna set against Christ’s earthly loaves.
Calvin: they extol the manna carnally to bury Christ’s doctrine; ‘bread of heaven’ (Ps 78:24) never meant God fed them ‘like swine.’
Wesley: the manna’s ‘heaven’ is the lower sky, opposed to the highest heaven from which Christ sevenfold ‘came down.’
Blunt denial that Moses gave the bread; ‘my Father’ (not ‘our’) and present ‘giveth’ (continual); the manna a type of the genuine (alēthinos) bread.
Calvin: the contrast is Moses vs Christ, not Moses vs God; the manna came from the visible sky, not the eternal kingdom from which life flows.
Lightfoot: against the rabbinic notion that manna came ‘for the merits of Moses,’ Christ denies Moses gave it at all.

outliers.note: Again redirects to the means of eternal life — faith and the preaching of the gospel.

schools.summary: The bread of God defined by heavenly origin and life-giving power; tight parallel with v.32; ‘coming down’ recurs 7×; manna gave trophē not zōē (Chrysostom).
Calvin: Christ reasons from the definition — the whole world is dead to God except as Christ quickens it; Wesley: life for the world, not one people.
Carnal literalism like the Samaritan woman’s ‘give me this water’ (4:15) — they want a perpetual supply, echoing the Jewish messianic-banquet hope.
Calvin: they speak ironically, to accuse Christ of vain boasting.
Wesley: they mean it literally, yet seem ‘not far from believing.’

debates.question: In what spirit do they ask for ‘this bread’?

sides.position: carnal misunderstanding, like the Samaritan woman
ironic mockery, accusing Christ of boasting
literal but nearly believing

schools.summary: The first ‘I am’ saying; living bread that has and gives life; ‘come’ and ‘believe’ are the appropriating acts answering to eating, satisfying every need.
Chrysostom: spoken first of Christ’s Divinity (the flesh comes later); Theophylact: bread of life, not mere nourishment — never weary of God’s word.
Augustine: to come is to believe; ‘never hunger’ and ‘never thirst’ both name the eternal society where there is no want.
Calvin: the food-metaphor lets us perceive Christ’s grace more deeply than a plain statement; Gill: he gives and sustains the life of dead sinners.

outliers.note: Refers ‘bread of life’ to Christ’s Divinity, reserving the bread = flesh of the later verses for the end of the discourse.

schools.summary: Refers (in substance) to v.26 or an unrecorded saying; ‘ye have even seen Me’ = fullest evidence, yet unbelief — answering their demand for a sign (v.30).
Calvin: inexcusable baseness to reject the Messiah they owned; the ‘seeing’ is voluntary blindness, malice darkened by Satan; yet (Henry) Christ’s work is not in vain — others will come.
